name: Ramsey Lewis
abstract: Ramsey Emmanuel Lewis Jr. (May 27, 1935 – September 12, 2022) was an American jazz pianist, composer, and radio personality.

interviews.title: Ramsey Lewis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: I think the only record he ever liked was Ramsey Lewis’ “The In Crowd.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Nancy Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Ramsey’s a lot of fun to work with... I enjoyed doing the album with him... This one, we were there together, and that was a lot of fun.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nancy Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Levy
about_quotes.evidence: Originally I did have agreements – written agreements with Ahmad Jamal, with Ramsey Lewis.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Håkan Rydin
about_quotes.evidence: Not so very much, yeah? Well of course I’ve heard it, that’s a great piano player.
about_quotes.doc_title: Håkan Rydin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Glasgow, Scotland, July 27, 2016

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: Ramsey Lewis was playing very much a cross between what I was doing with my trio and John Lewis was doing with the Modern jazz Quartet... Ramsey being smart enough to realize that these are the clubs he is playing now, developed his own thing...
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: Interviewer mentions Ramsey Lewis in the context of Chicago musicians; Interviewee does not mention a personal relationship with Ramsey Lewis, only noting that Larry Gray 'plays with Ramsey' and listing Ramsey as a prominent Chicago musician.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
